title: Data Centres Shift to 800V DC Architecture | datapod posted on the topic | LinkedIn
text:
The shift from AC to 800V DC is one of the most consequential architectural changes the data centre industry has seen in a generation — and it is happening faster than most operators are positioned for. A few signals from the past month worth sitting with: → At Data Centre World London this March, the conversation moved from "if" to "when." LS Electric put the headline number on the table: ~10% total power system loss in a DC architecture, versus ~25% in conventional AC. → NVIDIA's Vera Rubin Ultra Kyber platform is being designed natively around 800 VDC, with the broader power ecosystem mobilising around the reference design through the second half of 2026. → Texas Instruments and STMicroelectronics have both released full 800 VDC reference architectures aligned to NVIDIA's design — collapsing the conversion chain from grid to GPU into as few as two stages. → The driver is simple physics. A 1 MW rack at conventional voltage can require ~200 kg of copper busbar. A 1 GW site, 200 tonnes. At megawatt-per-rack densities, low-voltage distribution stops being an engineering choice and starts being a constraint. What this means for sovereign and defence buyers is worth saying plainly: the AI factory is no longer a compute problem with a power tail. It is a power architecture problem with a compute payload. Operators planning deployments into 2027 and beyond who specify around today's 415V AC topology will be retrofitting before their first refresh cycle. At Datapod, this is the architectural conversation we are having with every serious customer. Our Protean G3 HyperPod is engineered for the density curve the industry is now committing to — 200 kW per rack today, with a roadmap to 600 kW aligned to the Vera Rubin generation. Modular, liquid-cooled, and designed to be 800 VDC-ready as the ecosystem matures around Kyber. The window to design for what's coming — rather than retrofit to it — is now. #AIInfrastructure #DataCentres #SovereignAI #800VDC #AIFactory

      PCB vs Power Infrastructure: What’s Really Limiting AI Scaling? In most AI infrastructure discussions, the spotlight tends to fall on GPUs, accelerators, and cooling systems. But as systems scale, the limiting factor is becoming less obvious—and more structural. It’s increasingly difficult to separate compute performance from the infrastructure that supports it. PCB design, power delivery architecture, and upstream transformer systems are now operating as one connected constraint chain rather than independent engineering layers. At high power densities, small inefficiencies in the electrical path are no longer local issues. They propagate across the system, affecting signal stability, thermal headroom, and ultimately sustained compute performance. This is where an interesting divide is emerging in the industry conversation. One side focuses on advancing compute capability at the chip level. The other is dealing with the physical reality of delivering stable, efficient power into increasingly dense environments. In practice, neither side can scale independently. PCB design is being pushed into power integrity challenges it was never historically optimized for, while power infrastructure is being asked to respond to load dynamics that resemble data workloads rather than traditional electrical demand profiles. The result is a convergence: board-level design and facility-level power systems are starting to behave like a single optimization problem. The real question may not be “what limits AI scaling,” but rather: which layer of the stack becomes the first unavoidable bottleneck under extreme density conditions? #PCBDesign #ElectronicsEngineering #HardwareEngineering #PowerElectronics #SemiconductorIndustry #AIInfrastructure #DataCenters #HighPerformanceComputing #AIHardware #CloudInfrastructure To view or add a comment, sign in

      The next AI bottleneck is not the chip. It is the building standard behind the chip. NVIDIA's 800 VDC roadmap is built for 1 MW IT racks starting in 2027. Power-infrastructure vendors are already aligning around that shift. Most facilities, though, were designed in a 10-40kW rack language. Even 80-120kW still gets treated as the exception. From the infrastructure side, once you cross certain density thresholds, "we'll retrofit later" usually stops meaning retrofit. It starts meaning redesign: distribution, protection, cooling interface, floor layout, and commissioning sequence. My rough read: 2026 is the year this stops being an engineering curiosity and becomes an investment filter. If your facility standard still assumes yesterday's power density, the GPU roadmap is moving faster than the building. Are you treating 800 VDC as a future procurement choice, or as a current design assumption? #DataCenters #PowerInfrastructure #AIInfrastructure #ElectricalEngineering #Hyperscale To view or add a comment, sign in

      Traditional 54V power distribution just hit its wall. AI racks are now demanding 1 megawatt each. That's not a future problem — NVIDIA's Vera Rubin platform is in full production right now, and the Kyber platform, arriving in 2027, will fit 576 Rubin Ultra GPUs in a single rack. The data center industry is at a critical inflection point: traditional 54 VDC in-rack distribution, designed for kilowatt-scale racks, cannot meet the megawatt-scale demands of accelerated computing. Vertiv is engineering the answer — in collaboration with NVIDIA. And they aren't slowing down. 🔵 800 VDC portfolio launching H2 2026 — timed to Rubin Ultra rollout 🔵 Centralized rectifiers, DC busways, rack-level DC-DC converters, DC backup systems 🔵 +85% more power through the same copper vs. 480 VAC 🔵 +5% end-to-end efficiency by eliminating rack-level conversion losses 🔵 Validated in the NVIDIA Vera Rubin DSX AI factory reference design Vertiv's 800 VDC power portfolio is scheduled for release in the second half of 2026 — ahead of NVIDIA Kyber and NVIDIA Rubin Ultra platform rollouts. And the timeline is compressed in a way I haven't seen before — Vertiv went from concept to engineering-ready in under two years because NVIDIA's roadmap didn't give anyone the luxury of moving slowly. If you're designing, speccing, or procuring data center infrastructure right now and 800 VDC isn't in the conversation yet — it needs to be. H2 2026 is closer than it feels.       NVIDIA is talking about 5 megawatts per rack for highly specialized AI applications. Mitsubishi Electric's engineers at Data Centre World London put it plainly: "100 kW per rack was called crazy five years ago." The trajectory is real. The immediate operational challenge is different. Here is what is happening today in APAC data halls: - 120 to 132 kW per rack for NVIDIA GB200 NVL72. Liquid cooling is mandatory. No exceptions. - 250 kW per rack for Rittal's 1MW DTC pods. Entering early deployment. - 600 kW per rack for NVIDIA Rubin Ultra Kyber. Slated for 2027 in pre-spec stage. Each density step does not just require a different CDU. It requires a different commissioning model, a different fluid chemistry specification, and a different facility validation protocol. The operators getting this wrong are not failing on hardware selection. They are failing on the assumption that the commissioning playbook from their last project carries forward. Turner & Townsend's 2025 cost index confirms the structural shift. Liquid-cooled builds now allocate 33% of total facility cost to mechanical, versus 22% for air-cooled. That is not just a cost delta. It is a signal that the engineering complexity has fundamentally changed. APAC colo and hyperscaler operators: has your commissioning scope been rewritten for 120+ kW rack density, or is it still derived from your last air-cooled project? #LiquidCooling #DataCentre #AIInfrastructure #APAC #MissionCritical #ThermalManagement To view or add a comment, sign in

      Liquid Cooling: Managing the Thermal Limits of Next-Gen Silicon Pushing complex data analysis and continuous deep learning workflows through high-performance compute hardware fundamentally breaks legacy data center infrastructure. Dense clusters running architecture like Nvidia’s RTX 50-series GPUs, or enterprise server arrays packing Intel Core Ultra NPUs, generate extreme heat. Traditional air conditioning cannot physically remove 120+ kilowatts of heat per rack; direct-to-chip liquid cooling is now a mandatory requirement. Vertiv Holdings (NYSE: VRT): Vertiv is the dominant, pure-play proxy for AI infrastructure. They provide the highly specialized thermal management and uninterruptible power systems that hyperscalers rely on. Liquid cooling is now the default spec for new AI facilities, and Vertiv recently reported a staggering $15 billion+ project backlog, cementing its moat in the space. Modine Manufacturing (NYSE: MOD): Originally a legacy industrial thermal management company, Modine has aggressively pivoted its expertise into high-density data center cooling. Their "Airedale" brand is seeing massive sales growth specifically because it provides the specialized coolant distribution units required to keep next-generation silicon from melting down under maximum workload.       How much longer before the "impractical" becomes practical? We are passing bars before they have been set advancing paces that don't just exceed engineering principles they are going to have us rethinking the engineering and systems entirely. My best guess is that the first group that recreates or comes close to the limitless cold of outer space in a containable environment on earth with reasonable amounts of energy and natural resources will win... Maybe my thinking is to grandeos or "impossible" but one has to think bigger than the new norms approaching to truly be able to deal with them. Sorry I'm waxing philosphical with my MEP knowledge as if no one ever thought of the impossible then no one would have invented what got us to this point in our civilization. An apple fell, a key on a kite string, black goop leaking out of the ground caught fire, wires inside a glass "bulb", taps on one end crossing wires to tap on another, voices over wires, signals broadcast over air to metal antennas put people inside a bunch of vacuum tubes in a box in your house, microwave cooks food and so many other therories proven principles proven and I mean it was all considered "impossible" then it wasn't! Here is what AI has to say about the outer space temps to data center scale..after we talked and I asked does it have to be absolute outer space cold or what is a potential "good enough" point. Claude - So your instinct about “good enough” is exactly right — and 77K with liquid nitrogen is likely where a serious first-generation cryo data center would target. It’s almost achievable with today’s hardware, which is what makes it genuinely interesting as a near-term proposition. Principal Consultant at Préjean Consulting | AI Data Center Infrastructure · Cooling · Power Delivery · Mining-to-AI Conversion NVIDIA’s Rubin Ultra architecture is targeting 600kW per rack. Vera Rubin at 300kW is already a 2.5x jump over Blackwell. Ultra doubles it again. A 100,000 rack deployment at 600kW per rack needs 60GW of power capacity. That’s roughly the entire generating capacity of South Korea. For one campus. At that density, air cooling is physically impossible. Direct-to-chip liquid cooling isn’t optional, it’s the only path. But cooling is just the first problem. Every watt that reaches a rack has to pass through transformers at every step of the delivery chain. More watts per rack doesn’t mean more data centers. It means exponentially more electrical infrastructure per data center. Lead times on large power transformers are 2-4 years. The ERCOT interconnection queue already has 2,600MW waiting. And Rubin Ultra hasn’t even started shipping yet. The bottleneck isn’t chips. It’s everything downstream. To view or add a comment, sign in
